topic: A student learns their scholarship application was denied
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email sat on the screen, stark white against the dark background of my laptop. My fingers hovered over the 'delete' button, then slowly drifted back to the subject line: "Scholarship Application Update." A cold knot tightened in my stomach, spreading outwards with alarming speed. I reread the rejection letter three times, the carefully chosen words blurring into an incomprehensible mess. My breath hitched, shallow and quick in my chest. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal on the floor sounding deafeningly loud in the quiet dorm room. I walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent campus quad, the vibrant autumn leaves seeming mocking in their brilliance. Every muscle in my jaw felt tight, a constant, dull ache. I needed to breathe, but the air felt thin, insufficient.

[story 2]

I kept replaying the interview in my head, searching for the misstep, the moment I faltered. The rejection notice lay crumpled on my desk, the crisp paper feeling heavy and oppressive in my hands. My hands trembled slightly as I smoothed it out again, tracing the lines of the decision. A strange, hollow feeling settled deep in my core, a profound sense of deflation. I paced the small confines of my apartment, running a hand through my already messy hair. Each step seemed clumsy, uncoordinated. I stopped by the mirror, observing my reflection—pale, eyes wide, a tightness around my mouth that refused to relax. I wanted to shout, to argue, but the sound caught in my throat, leaving only a dry, rasping silence.

[story 3]

The phone buzzed incessantly on the bedside table, each vibration a sharp jolt to my already frayed nerves. I ignored it, focusing instead on the stack of rejected materials sitting on my nightstand. When I finally picked up the letter, the paper felt brittle, almost fragile. My vision tunneled slightly; the room seemed to tilt. I reread the statement about insufficient merit, the words echoing hollowly in the silence of the room. A sudden wave of nausea washed over me, making me grip the edge of the mattress until my knuckles turned white. I squeezed my eyes shut, trying to force a steady rhythm back into my breathing, but the effort felt futile, exhausting.
